speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome to Git Going with GitHub, episode 32: GitHub Mobile. I am Alex. Today we are going to make GitHub Mobile something you can explain, practice, and recover from when the interface surprises you.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And I am Jamie. I will be the voice of the learner who is willing to ask, what is this for, where am I, and how do I know I did it right?

speaker: ALEX
text: The big idea today: VoiceOver and TalkBack guide for iOS and Android GitHub apps. We will name the concept, explain why it matters, practice the move, and point out the checks that tell you the work is real.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the episode should work even if someone has not read the chapter yet.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The transcript has to stand on its own. It can point toward practice, but it should teach the concept right here in the conversation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay, set the room for us. What are we walking into?

speaker: ALEX
text: Accessibility Guide for iOS and Android: GitHub Mobile brings issues, pull requests, notifications, and code review to your iPhone, iPad, or Android device. The next useful detail is concrete: This appendix covers setup, VoiceOver and TalkBack usage, and the tasks best suited to mobile.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next layer is this. Learning Cards: GitHub Mobile Overview has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here are the anchors worth keeping. GitHub Mobile supports VoiceOver (iOS) and TalkBack (Android) natively -- swipe right to move through elements, double-tap to activate. The app uses five bottom tabs (Home, Notifications, Explore, Pull Requests, Profile) -- swipe left or right along the bottom tab bar to switch between them. Use the VoiceOver Rotor (two-finger twist) set to Headings to jump between sections within an issue or PR description. GitHub Mobile supports dynamic text sizing on both iOS and Android -- increase your system font size and the app respects it. Dark mode is available in the app settings and follows your system preference -- use it to reduce glare on OLED screens. The simplified diff view in Files Changed shows additions and removals as text only, without the two-column table layout used on desktop.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the one idea that makes the next few steps less mysterious?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside 1. Installing GitHub Mobile: after installing, sign in with your GitHub account. That matters in practice: Enable notifications when prompted - these are essential for staying on top of PR reviews and issue activity without constantly checking the web.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That feels much more doable when you say it as one move.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The magic is not speed. The magic is knowing what changed and why it matters.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now bring the learner back to the room. Anchor this part on 2. Getting Around the App. GitHub Mobile is organized into five main tabs at the bottom of the screen. This is the part to say slowly: Navigate between tabs with a single tap (or swipe on iOS with VoiceOver active).

speaker: JAMIE
text: What would you say to someone who is already bracing for this to be too much?

speaker: ALEX
text: Enabling VoiceOver has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The parts worth keeping in working memory are these. Triple-click the side button (iPhone X and later) or triple-click the Home button to toggle VoiceOver. Or: Settings → Accessibility → VoiceOver → toggle on.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful habit is simple: orient, act, verify, then continue. That pause between action and trust is part of the work.

speaker: ALEX
text: That matters because of the next idea. Do not treat Rotor in GitHub Mobile as decoration. Open the Rotor by rotating two fingers on the screen as if turning a dial. That is not trivia. Useful rotor settings for GitHub Mobile. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: ALEX
text: On the ground, that means a few things. Headings - jump between section headings on an issue or PR description. Links - navigate to linked issues, commits, or external URLs. Form Controls - jump to input fields when writing a comment. Actions - available actions for the focused element (assign, label, close).

speaker: JAMIE
text: How would you walk the room through that step by step?

speaker: ALEX
text: Writing Comments with VoiceOver has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Navigate to the comment field (swipe to it or use Rotor → Form Controls). Then: Double tap to activate and open the keyboard. Next: Type your comment; VoiceOver announces each character. Last: When done, swipe to the Comment button and double tap to submit. The sequence works because every action has a confirmation.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the talk moves from concept to action. Enabling TalkBack has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The room should hear these as checkpoints. Settings → Accessibility → TalkBack → toggle on. Or: hold both volume keys for three seconds (if the shortcut is enabled).

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now it sounds like a workflow instead of a wall of instructions.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is where confidence comes from: not from never getting lost, but from knowing how to recover.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Where do you want a learner to place their attention here?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Tap with three fingers (or swipe down then right) to open the TalkBack menu.

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the details that keep the idea from floating away. Change the reading granularity (character, word, line, paragraph). Activate reading controls. Copy text.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the ordered workflow?

speaker: ALEX
text: Writing Comments with TalkBack has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is locate the comment field - TalkBack announces "Edit text, double tap to edit". Step two is double tap to enter the field. Step three is use the on-screen keyboard or dictation to type. Step four is locate the Comment button and double tap to submit. The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the version that sounds like an instructor, not a manual.

speaker: ALEX
text: 5. Working with Notifications: GitHub Mobile's Notifications tab is one of its strongest features for AT users - it surfaces all activity in a clean, linear list that is much easier to navigate than the GitHub web notifications page.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid project habit is to treat metadata as decision support. Labels, status, assignees, and notifications tell you what kind of attention the work needs.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now slow down for the part people usually miss. Learning Cards: Mobile Notifications has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part that makes the next action easier. The Notifications tab presents a linear list that is more accessible than the web version -- swipe right through items one by one to hear each notification's context. Filter controls are accessible form elements at the top -- navigate to the filter icon, activate it, then swipe through filter options (Type, Repository, Reason). Swipe actions (left to archive, right to mark done) work with VoiceOver custom actions -- use the VoiceOver Actions rotor item on each notification. Notifications are displayed as a vertical list with the repository name, notification type, and title on each row -- text is sized according to your system accessibility settings. The filter panel overlays on top of the list -- look for the funnel icon in the top-right corner of the Notifications tab. Unread notifications appear with a blue dot indicator on the left edge of each row.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If I am listening before the workshop starts, what should settle in my mind first?

speaker: ALEX
text: Inbox Management has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the pieces that turn the idea into a usable move. Swipe left on a notification to reveal quick actions: Mark as read, Archive, Unsubscribe. Swipe right to mark as done. Tap a notification to open the full issue or PR.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the kind of detail that keeps a screen reader user oriented.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The named thing - the heading, tab, field, branch, or button - is the handhold.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the moment where the page starts to make sense. Anchor this part on Filtering Notifications. Use the filter icon at the top right to filter. This is the part to say slowly: With VoiceOver or TalkBack, the filter controls are accessible form elements. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Listen for the small confirmations in this list. Type (Issues, PRs, Releases, etc.). Repository. Reason (you were @mentioned, a review was requested, etc.).

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 6. Reviewing Pull Requests. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: mobile is well suited for quick PR reviews - approving straightforward changes, leaving a comment, or checking CI status while away from your desk.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid pull request habit is to separate three questions: what changed, why it changed, and what still needs review. That keeps the conversation useful instead of noisy.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next point gives the learner a handle. Do not treat Navigating a PR as decoration. When you open a pull request, the screen is divided into sections. That is not trivia. VoiceOver and TalkBack announce these as headings.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the lesson becomes something you can check. Description - the PR body with any images or checklists. Commits - individual commits in this PR. Files changed - a simplified diff view. Checks - CI/CD status.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does the learner do first, second, and then after that?

speaker: ALEX
text: Leaving a Review has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Scroll to or navigate to the Review changes button. Then: Double tap to open the review panel. Next: Choose Approve, Request changes, or Comment. Last: Add an optional comment in the text field. Pause after each step and listen for the confirmation before moving on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Turn that into a path someone can follow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Activate Submit review. The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: ALEX
text: Hold that next to this. Put Viewing Diffs into plain language. The Files Changed tab shows a simplified diff - additions and removals are text-only (no table layout). The useful version is: Each changed line is announced as "Added: [content]" or "Removed: [content]", which is generally more accessible than the web diff table on small screens. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the learner is not behind if they stop there and check the page.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Pausing to verify is not a detour; it is how you keep control of the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the sequence, because order matters here.

speaker: ALEX
text: Filing an Issue has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 4 checks: Navigate to the repository; Tap Issues → the + button or New Issue; Fill in the title and body; and Optionally assign labels, assignees, and milestone - each is a tappable field. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Filing an Issue. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is tap Submit. If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid issue habit is to read the title, the body, and the timeline before acting. You are listening for the requested action, the missing evidence, and the person who needs a response.

speaker: ALEX
text: That connects to another useful point. Finding Your Issues has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: For a learner, the useful signals are concrete. Pull Requests tab → Created by you or Assigned to you filters. For issues: Home feed shows recent activity; or navigate to a specific repository → Issues → filter by Assignee, Label, or Author.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What stays the same when the tool changes?

speaker: ALEX
text: 8. What Mobile Does Well vs. Desktop: Best use of GitHub Mobile: notification triage, quick approvals and comments, catching up on activity between sessions. The next useful detail is concrete: For writing substantial code, descriptions, or reviewing complex diffs, use the web or VS Code.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the practical turn. Here is the learner-facing version. Long PR descriptions with images, tables, or embedded videos may not read cleanly. Put another way, open the PR in Safari instead - tap the … menu → Open in Browser. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on TalkBack does not announce new notifications badge. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside TalkBack does not announce new notifications badge: the badge count on the Notifications tab updates live but may not be announced. That matters in practice: Navigate directly to the Notifications tab to get the current count read aloud.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a useful checkpoint before anyone starts pressing keys.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Checkpoints turn uncertainty into information.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the thread going. Anchor this part on The keyboard covers the comment field. Usual iOS/Android behavior - scroll up slightly after the keyboard appears, or rotate to landscape mode to gain more visible space.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid navigation habit is to prove where you are before activating controls. Headings, landmarks, and the address bar are not trivia; they are your map.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Can't find the Submit button after writing a review. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: scroll down past the text field; buttons are below the keyboard dismiss area. The listener should be able to check this: On iOS, tap elsewhere to dismiss the keyboard first, then scroll to Submit.

speaker: ALEX
text: Another way to ground it. Do not treat GitHub Mobile crashes or freezes as decoration. If the problem persists, sign out and back in via Profile → Settings → Sign out. That is not trivia. Next: Appendix W: GitHub Pages Back: Appendix U: Discussions and Gists Teaching chapter: Chapter 05: Working with Issues. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should people carry with them after this?

speaker: ALEX
text: Carry the map. Know what page or tool you are in, know which action you are taking, and know what confirmation should follow. If the confirmation is missing, pause. That pause is not wasted time; it is professional judgment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a better way to say it than just follow the steps.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Steps matter, but understanding wins. That is episode 32. Next in the series is episode 33, where we keep building the same contributor muscles.
